title: David Copperfield
author: Charles Dickens
overture: David Copperfield lives a whole life across this map, from a Suffolk churchyard where he is born to a Swiss valley where he learns to bear his griefs. Between them lie the boat-house at Yarmouth, the blacking warehouse and the courts of London, the long boy's walk to Dover, the quiet of Canterbury — and, at the map's far edges, Naples and a new world in Australia.

sources.name: The Charles Dickens Page — David Copperfield
Blundeston — the village and the Copperfield connection (Kent / Suffolk parish records)
The Victorian Web — Doctors' Commons and the law in Dickens

characters.name: David Copperfield
characters.role: The narrator, telling his own life from birth: Blunderstone boy, warehouse drudge, Canterbury scholar, London proctor, and at last a writer — the thread the whole map is strung on

characters.name: Little Em'ly
characters.role: Mr Peggotty's niece, David's boyhood sweetheart — the fisher-girl who dreams of being a lady, elopes with Steerforth to her ruin, is found and reclaimed, and sails for a new life in Australia

characters.name: James Steerforth
characters.role: David's dazzling, careless schoolfriend from Highgate; he charms the Peggottys, carries Em'ly off, and is drowned in the Yarmouth storm within sight of the home he wrecked

characters.name: Mr Peggotty
characters.role: The Yarmouth fisherman in the boat-house; when Em'ly is carried off he crosses France and Italy on foot in a years-long search, brings her home, and emigrates with her

characters.name: The Micawbers
characters.role: Wilkins Micawber and his loyal wife — genteel, indebted, endlessly hopeful; from London lodgings to Heep's clerkship in Canterbury, and finally to unlikely eminence in Australia

locations.name: Blundeston, Suffolk
locations.note: Blundeston, near Lowestoft: Dickens saw the signpost between Yarmouth and Lowestoft in January 1849 while planning the book, and lifted the name (softening it to Blunderstone). The Rookery house itself is invented.
locations.story: David's birthplace, and the whole first act of his childhood: born here six months after his father's death, he grows up between his gentle mother and stern Peggotty, until the Murdstones arrive to marry his mother and break the house up. He is sent away, comes home to find his mother dead, and never lives here again.
locations.quote: I was born at Blunderstone, in Suffolk

locations.name: Great Yarmouth
locations.note: Great Yarmouth is a real town. The Peggottys' home — an upturned barge beached on the Denes, made into a house — is Dickens's invention on the real flats east of the town.
locations.story: The flat, sea-smelling town where Peggotty's family live in a black boat drawn up on the shore. David is happiest here as a boy; it is the home of Little Em'ly and Ham, and the harbour Mr Peggotty keeps returning to. It is also where the storm comes, at the very end.
locations.quote: There was a black barge, or some other kind of superannuated boat, not far off, high and dry on the ground, with an iron funnel sticking out of it for a chimney and smoking very cosily

locations.name: Blackheath, London
locations.note: Mr Creakle's school is placed 'down by Blackheath' in the text; the exact building is invented, so this point stands on Blackheath, south-east London.
locations.story: The brutal boarding school run by the ignorant, caning Mr Creakle, where David is made to wear a placard reading 'Take care of him. He bites.' Here he meets the two poles of his life: gentle, doomed Traddles and the dazzling, careless Steerforth, who takes him up as a favourite.

locations.name: Blackfriars, London
locations.note: A real quarter of riverside London. The wine-bottling warehouse is modelled closely on Warren's Blacking factory at Hungerford Stairs, where the twelve-year-old Dickens himself was sent to labour — the buried wound of his own childhood.
locations.story: The riverside warehouse where the ten-year-old David is set to washing and labelling bottles among rough boys, lodging with the Micawbers. It is the pit of the novel: shameful, hopeless drudgery that David never quite forgives, and the source of Dickens's own lifelong grievance.
locations.quote:
Murdstone and Grinby's warehouse was at the waterside. It was down in
Blackfriars.

locations.name: Windsor Terrace, City Road
locations.note: City Road is a real London street; the Micawbers' particular lodging is Dickens's placing, folded onto it here.
locations.story: The Micawbers' shabby-genteel lodging, where David boards during the warehouse days. Mr Micawber waits here, magnificently, for something to turn up, dodging creditors between bursts of oratory — before debt carries him off to the King's Bench prison.
locations.quote: My address,' said Mr. Micawber, 'is Windsor Terrace, City Road.

locations.name: Canterbury
locations.note: A real cathedral city. Mr Wickfield's house and Dr Strong's school are inventions placed within it.
locations.story: The gentle counterweight to London — though David first comes through it wayworn and ragged on his walk to Dover. Later Betsey Trotwood sends him to school at Dr Strong's here, boarding with the lawyer Mr Wickfield and his daughter Agnes, the quiet centre of David's whole life. It is also where the writhing clerk Uriah Heep spins his web, and where the Micawbers come as his tools.
locations.quote:
some of the boys had seen me coming through
Canterbury, wayworn and ragged

image.caption: Canterbury Cathedral, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom · Library of Congress · public domain

locations.name: Dover
locations.note: The text places Betsey Trotwood 'near Dover'. The cliff-top cottage itself is invented; Dickens is generally thought to have drawn it from 2 Nuckell's Place at Broadstairs, further up the Kent coast — so the town is Dover, but the real-world model is elsewhere. The map trusts the text.
locations.story: The end of David's desperate walk from London: footsore and ragged, he finds his aunt Betsey Trotwood in her neat cottage above the sea. She takes him in, sees off the pursuing Murdstones, and — for the first time since his mother died — he is safe.

locations.name: Highgate
locations.note: A real village on the hill north of London. Both the Steerforths' house and, later, David and Dora's cottage are inventions placed here.
locations.story: On its hilltop: first the proud Mrs Steerforth's house, where David visits his charming friend and meets the watchful Rosa Dartle; and later the cottage where David brings his child-wife Dora, and where their sweet, hopeless attempt at housekeeping plays out.
locations.quote: old brick house at Highgate on the summit of the hill

locations.name: Doctors' Commons, near St Paul's
locations.note: A real legal quarter near St Paul's, the seat of the ecclesiastical and admiralty courts. It was a genuine institution, wound up and demolished in 1867 — Dickens describes it as an antique survival even in the 1840s.
locations.story: The 'lazy old nook' of church-law courts where David is articled as a proctor, buying his way into a profession. Here he falls headlong for Dora Spenlow, his employer's daughter, and here he later scrapes for a living when his aunt's fortune collapses.
locations.quote:
some faded courts held in Doctors' Commons,--a lazy old nook near St.
Paul's Churchyard

locations.name: Putney
locations.note: A real riverside district south-west of London. Dora's aunts' house is invented and placed here.
locations.story: Where Dora's spinster aunts take her after her father's sudden death, and where David haunts the road to court her. It is from Putney that he finally wins her, and marries her.
locations.quote: maiden sisters of Mr. Spenlow, who lived at Putney

locations.name: the Golden Cross, Charing Cross
locations.note: A real coaching inn at Charing Cross, terminus of the Dover road. The original was demolished in 1827 to clear the way for Trafalgar Square; Dickens writes it as the 'mouldy' establishment of an earlier day.
locations.story: The coaching inn where David, now seventeen, puts up on returning to London a young gentleman — and by chance meets the grown-up Steerforth again, the friendship rekindling just as it will lead them both to Yarmouth, and to ruin.
locations.quote:
We went to the Golden Cross at Charing Cross, then a mouldy sort of
establishment in a close neighbourhood.

locations.name: Naples
locations.note: The real city. The text is explicit: Steerforth and Em'ly keep a villa 'in the neighbourhood of Naples', and it is from there that he abandons her.
locations.story: The bright southern shore where Steerforth carries Little Em'ly after their elopement, and where — grown tired of her — he leaves her, coldly offering her in marriage to his servant. It is the far point of her ruin, and the turn from which she begins the long road home.
locations.quote:
from the neighbourhood of
Naples, where we had a villa

locations.name: Gravesend
locations.note: A real town on the Thames estuary, the traditional point where emigrant ships took on passengers below London.
locations.story: The Thames-side port where the emigrant ship lies waiting. Here David watches the whole broken company he loves — Mr Peggotty and the reclaimed Em'ly, the Micawbers, poor Martha — go aboard to sail for Australia and a new life, and waves them out towards the sea.
locations.quote:
my old nurse and I went down to
Gravesend. We found the ship in the river

locations.name: near Melbourne, Australia
locations.note: Dickens's invented Australian port. Scholars place it in the Melbourne region by consensus — the colony to which mid-century emigrants of this kind sailed — but the novel never fixes it, so this point stands at Melbourne.
locations.story: The far side of the world, where the emigrants make good. Mr Peggotty and Em'ly settle to a hard, honest life; and Mr Micawber, of all men, comes into his own at last — 'Port Middlebay District Magistrate', toasted at a public dinner, the eternal debtor turned pillar of a colony.
locations.quote: WILKINS MICAWBER, ESQUIRE, Port Middlebay District Magistrate

locations.name: a valley in the Alps, near Geneva
locations.note: The text names only 'a valley' in the Swiss Alps, reached over one of the great passes out of Italy; it is never located. Geneva is the association most often drawn by scholars, so this point stands in the Alps south of it. An honest approximation.
locations.story: Where David, crushed by Dora's death and the drowning of Steerforth and Ham, wanders in grief for months. In a still mountain valley, opening a bundle of letters from Agnes, his numbness finally breaks — and he begins, slowly, to live again, and to write.
locations.quote:
I was in Switzerland. I had come out of Italy, over one of the great
passes of the Alps

movements.note: The childhood fortnight with Peggotty's family — Barkis's cart down to the sea
Home again — to find his mother married to Mr Murdstone in his absence

via.name: Ipswich
via.note: the great coaching town on the London road

via.name: Romford
via.note: last stage into London from the east

movements.routeNote: The old coach road from the Yarmouth/Lowestoft country up to London - through Ipswich, Colchester, Chelmsford and Romford - here bending round the eastern edge of the City to Salem House at Blackheath.
movements.routeSource: Cary's New Itinerary / the Great Essex Road (London-Colchester-Ipswich-Norwich turnpike), the standard coaching route between London and East Anglia in the coaching era.
movements.note: Sent away to Mr Creakle's school after biting Murdstone; the long stage-coach up to Blackheath
Home for the holidays — and, soon after, for his mother's death and the breaking-up of the house

via.name: Ipswich
via.note: the great coaching town on the London road

via.name: Romford
via.note: last stage into London from the east

movements.routeNote: The old coach road from the Yarmouth/Lowestoft country up to London - through Ipswich, Colchester, Chelmsford and Romford - into the City.
movements.routeSource: Cary's New Itinerary / the Great Essex Road (London-Colchester-Ipswich-Norwich turnpike), the standard coaching route between London and East Anglia in the coaching era.
movements.note: Cast out by Murdstone to labour at the riverside warehouse, and to lodge with the Micawbers

via.name: Blackheath
via.note: sleeps in the corner by his old school, Salem House

via.name: Rochester
via.note: over the Medway bridge — 'three-and-twenty miles' from London

via.name: Chatham
via.note: sleeps on a battery; sells his jacket to the mad old clothes-man next morning

via.name: Canterbury
via.note: the grey cathedral he carries with him ever after

movements.routeNote: David's runaway walk to his aunt at Dover — robbed in London at the very outset, then down the old Dover Road through Kent, sleeping by his old school at Blackheath, over Rochester bridge and a battery at Chatham, on past Canterbury to Dover on the sixth day.
movements.routeSource: Dickens narrates the walk stage by stage, chs. 12-13: robbed at the King's Bench prison wall before he is even clear of London; Blackheath, Rochester bridge, the battery at Chatham, the grey cathedral of Canterbury, and Dover on the sixth day.
movements.note: The famous flight: robbed on the first day, he walks the whole Dover road, over Rochester bridge and into Chatham, through Canterbury and out onto the downs, selling his clothes as he goes
Betsey Trotwood settles him at Dr Strong's school, boarding with the Wickfields

via.name: Dartford
via.note: last Kentish stage before the London river-crossing

movements.routeNote: The London-bound Dover Road from Canterbury - through Faversham, Chatham and Rochester on the Medway, then Dartford and over Blackheath to Charing Cross.
movements.routeSource: Cary's New Itinerary / Paterson's Roads: the London-Dover turnpike via Dartford, Rochester, Chatham, Canterbury - the principal road to the Continent.
movements.note: Schooldays over, a young gentleman sets out to see the world; he puts up at the Golden Cross and falls in again with Steerforth

via.name: Romford
via.note: out of London on the Great Essex Road

via.name: Norwich
via.note: the East Anglian capital; road turns east for the coast

movements.routeNote: The old Norwich road out of London - through Romford, Chelmsford, Colchester and Ipswich to Norwich, then east to Great Yarmouth on the coast.
movements.routeSource: Cary's New Itinerary / the London-Colchester-Ipswich-Norwich-Yarmouth coaching route, the standard road to East Anglia.
movements.note: He takes Steerforth down to Yarmouth to meet the Peggottys — the fateful introduction
Back to London to be articled as a proctor in Doctors' Commons, where he meets Dora Spenlow
Courting Dora at her aunts' house after her father's sudden death
Married; he carries his child-wife home to their little cottage, and their doomed housekeeping begins
After Dora's death, he sees the emigrants aboard their ship — and, half-broken, resolves to leave England himself

via.name: Dijon
via.note: up the road toward the Jura and Switzerland

via.name: Geneva
via.note: the one Swiss city Dickens names

movements.routeNote: Cross the Channel from Dover to Calais, overland through Paris and Dijon, then into the Bernese Alps - the conventional 1840s tourist route to Switzerland.
movements.routeSource: No route named in the text - Dickens simply sends David to wander in Switzerland during his grief. This reconstructs the standard mid-century Channel-Paris-Alps tourist itinerary (Murray's/Baedeker-era).
movements.note: Abroad to grieve; over the Alps out of Italy into a still Swiss valley, where Agnes's letters finally reach him and his numbness lifts

via.name: Geneva
via.note: the one Swiss city Dickens names

via.name: Dijon
via.note: down through the Jura toward Paris

movements.routeNote: The return: down out of the Bernese Alps through Dijon and Paris to Calais, across the Channel to Dover, then the short road inland to Canterbury.
movements.routeSource: No route named in the text - reconstructs the standard mid-century Alps-Paris-Channel tourist itinerary in reverse. Dickens brings David home to England.
movements.note: Home at last, three years on, to Agnes at Canterbury — the settled end
Down to Yarmouth with David, where the Peggottys take him to their hearts — and he fixes on Em'ly

via.name: Romford
via.note: out of London on the Great Essex Road

via.name: Norwich
via.note: the East Anglian capital; road turns east for the coast

movements.routeNote: The old Norwich road out of London - through Romford, Chelmsford, Colchester and Ipswich to Norwich, then east to Great Yarmouth on the coast.
movements.routeSource: Cary's New Itinerary / the London-Colchester-Ipswich-Norwich-Yarmouth coaching route, the standard road to East Anglia.

via.name: off the North Foreland
via.note: down the North Sea coast of Kent

via.name: the Dover Strait
via.note: through the narrows into the Channel

via.name: off Ushant
via.note: clearing the Channel for the Bay of Biscay

via.name: off Cape Finisterre
via.note: across Biscay to the NW corner of Spain

movements.routeNote: Down the North Sea past the Foreland, through the Dover Strait and English Channel, out past Ushant across the Bay of Biscay, down off Finisterre and the Portuguese coast, through the Strait of Gibraltar and across the western Mediterranean into the Tyrrhenian Sea to Naples.
movements.routeSource: Standard age-of-sail North Europe to Mediterranean sailing track (Channel - Biscay - off Finisterre/Portugal - Gibraltar - Tyrrhenian). Dickens names Naples as the destination of Steerforth and Em'ly's flight abroad.
movements.note: He carries Little Em'ly off across the sea to the villa near Naples
His yacht is driven back onto the Yarmouth sands in the great storm; he and Ham drown within sight of the boat-house

movements.routeSource: Standard age-of-sail North Europe to Mediterranean sailing track (Channel - Biscay - off Finisterre/Portugal - Gibraltar - Tyrrhenian). Dickens names Naples as the destination of Steerforth and Em'ly's flight abroad.
movements.note: The elopement: she leaves a letter and is gone with Steerforth to the south

movements.routeNote: The reverse passage: out of the Bay of Naples across the western Mediterranean, through the Strait of Gibraltar, up off Portugal and Finisterre, across Biscay past Ushant, up the English Channel and Dover Strait into the Thames Estuary to Gravesend.
movements.routeSource: Mirror of the Yarmouth-to-Naples track; Mr Peggotty brings Em'ly home from the Continent in the novel. Standard Mediterranean to Thames age-of-sail route.
movements.note: Abandoned, she makes her way back alone — France, and service abroad — until Martha and Mr Peggotty find her hidden in London and bring her to the emigrant ship

movements.routeSource: Standard age-of-sail North Europe to Mediterranean sailing track (Channel - Biscay - off Finisterre/Portugal - Gibraltar - Tyrrhenian). Dickens names Naples as the destination of Steerforth and Em'ly's flight abroad.
movements.note: The years-long search on foot: across-channel to France 'as if I'd fell down from the sky', then over into Italy, town by town, as far as the Swiss mountains — the path here is illustrative, the novel naming only France, Italy and the mountains

movements.note: Home to London with the found Em'ly, and on to the emigrant ship at Gravesend
Mr Micawber, ever hopeful, takes a confidential clerkship under Uriah Heep at Canterbury
He turns on his master in a scene of glorious denunciation, exposing Heep's frauds — then, ruined creditors settled, joins the emigrants

via.name: off the Isle of Wight
via.note: down the English Channel from the Thames

via.name: off Ushant
via.note: clearing the mouth of the Channel into the Atlantic

via.name: Madeira
via.note: standard refreshment call to catch the NE trades

via.name: toward the Brazilian coast
via.note: crossing the SE trades near the Brazil bulge

via.name: South Atlantic westerlies
via.note: curving SE off the River Plate longitude

via.name: off the Cape of Good Hope
via.note: Cape Town, the standard refreshment call; many 1840s ships ran on but the track still bent round the Cape

via.name: Southern Indian Ocean
via.note: running the easting down on the westerlies, north of 40 deg S per Admiralty advice

via.name: SW of Cape Leeuwin
via.note: moderate latitudes, NOT the extreme 50s of the later clipper route

via.name: off King Island
via.note: closing the Bass Strait approaches, turning NE for Port Phillip Heads

movements.routeNote: The 1840s pre-clipper emigrant passage: down the Channel and out past Ushant, SW to Madeira for the NE trades, across toward the Brazilian coast, SE to round the Cape of Good Hope, then east across the Southern Indian Ocean on the westerlies (moderate ~39-40 deg S, north of the 40th parallel as the Admiralty advised), up to Port Phillip.
movements.routeSource: State Library of Victoria, 'Ships' journeys' research guide, and ANU/CEPR economic-history work on migration to Australia (Hatton et al.): early-1840s voyages sailed south toward Brazil then SE, often calling at Cape Town, crossing the Indian Ocean keeping north of 40 deg S per Admiralty recommendation. The deep Roaring-Forties great-circle route was only promoted from ~1852 (Towson's tables, the Marco Polo era) and post-dates the novel.
movements.note: The long emigrant voyage to Australia: the whole reclaimed company sails together to begin a new life

story.title: A posthumous child
story.narration: David Copperfield is born at Blunderstone in Suffolk, six months after his father's death, adored by his gentle young mother and her fierce servant, Peggotty.

story.title: To the sea
story.narration: Peggotty takes the boy to Yarmouth, to her brother's boat-house, an upturned barge made a home on the flats. There David meets Little Em'ly, the fisherman's orphan niece, and loves her.

story.title: A changed house
story.narration: Home again, everything changed: his mother has married Mr Murdstone, a cold man of 'firmness', and the house goes quiet around the boy.

story.title: Sent away
story.narration: Beaten, David bites the hand that beats him and is packed off to Salem House, a London boarding school, placarded: 'Take care of him. He bites.'

story.title: Steerforth
story.narration: Salem House is Mr Creakle and his cane. David's consolation is James Steerforth, older, dazzling, careless, who takes him up as a favourite. Remember him.

story.title: The holidays
story.narration: Home for the holidays, to a mother grown timid and a new baby brother. It is the last time he sees her.

story.title: The empty house
story.narration: The news comes on his birthday: his mother is dead, her baby with her. David buries them; Peggotty is dismissed; the Murdstones have no more use for him.

story.title: Cast out
story.narration: Ten years old, he is sent to wash bottles at Murdstone's riverside warehouse, lodging with the Micawbers: grand of speech, hopeless with money, expecting something to turn up.

story.title: The pit
story.narration: The warehouse is shameful, hopeless drudgery. When debt carries Mr Micawber to prison, David resolves to run: to Dover, to an aunt he has never met.

story.title: The walk to Dover
story.narration: Robbed of box and money before he is clear of London, David walks the whole Dover road: six days, sleeping in fields, selling clothes for bread, through Rochester and Canterbury, over the downs, on the strength of one hope, Aunt Betsey.

story.title: Aunt Betsey
story.narration: Betsey Trotwood, brisk and kind under the crust, takes him in and sees off the Murdstones. For the first time since his mother died, he is safe.

story.title: A good school
story.narration: She sends him to school in Canterbury, a good one this time, boarding with her lawyer Mr Wickfield and his daughter Agnes.

story.title: Agnes — and Uriah
story.narration: Calm years: school, and Agnes Wickfield, the quiet centre of David's life. In the same house coils Uriah Heep, a 'humble' clerk with plans. David grows up.

story.title: Seventeen
story.narration: Schooldays done, seventeen, David coaches up to London, and at the Golden Cross inn walks into Steerforth again, now a splendid young man about town.

story.title: Two worlds meet
story.narration: David brings his dazzling friend Steerforth down to Yarmouth, to show him the boat-house: his two worlds, the gentleman and the fisherfolk, meeting at last. They should never have met.

story.title: The charmer
story.narration: Steerforth charms the boat-house whole — stays for weeks, buys a boat, is taken to every heart. Em'ly, newly engaged to her cousin Ham, cannot stop watching him.

story.title: A profession
story.narration: David returns to London to be a proctor in the drowsy church-courts of Doctors' Commons. Steerforth stays behind at Yarmouth, with his boat.

story.title: Dora — and ruin
story.narration: Young manhood: David falls helplessly for Dora Spenlow, his employer's childish, pretty daughter. Then his aunt's fortune collapses: mornings at shorthand, courting on nothing.
Wind the clock back. While David was falling in love, a letter was opened at Yarmouth, on the eve of Em'ly's wedding.

story.title: The elopement
story.narration: Em'ly is gone with Steerforth — 'to come back a lady', her letter pleads, or never — carried by sea to a villa near Naples. Ham is left at the altar's edge; her uncle sits all night by the fire.

story.title: Something turns up
story.narration: The Micawbers are moving too: Mr Micawber has taken a confidential clerkship in Canterbury, under, of all masters, Uriah Heep.

story.title: The candle in the window
story.narration: Mr Peggotty shuts the boat-house and sets a candle in the window for Em'ly. He means to find her, however far, however long.

story.title: The search
story.narration: The great search: across the Channel, down through France, over the mountains into Italy, town by town toward Naples. Years of walking, an old fisherman asking for his niece in languages he does not speak.
While he walks, wind back to London, where David, poor and busy, is still in love.

story.title: Courting Dora
story.narration: Dora's father dies suddenly; she is taken in by her maiden aunts at Putney. David wears out the road there, and wins her.

story.title: The child-wife
story.narration: Married. He carries his child-wife home to a cottage at Highgate, where housekeeping defeats them both, sweetly and completely. He loves her; he worries.

story.title: Abandoned
story.narration: Far south, the villa has gone quiet: Steerforth, bored with what he ruined, abandons Em'ly, offering her in parting insult to his servant. She flees.

story.title: Her own way home
story.narration: Em'ly makes her own long way home: service and hiding in France, then by sea to the Thames, vanishing into London, too ashamed to be found.

story.title: Found
story.narration: Word reaches Mr Peggotty and he comes home close behind. In a London lodging, uncle and niece are found and forgiven. He will take her where the past cannot follow: Australia.

story.title: Heep undone
story.narration: One score to settle: in Canterbury Mr Micawber rises and denounces his master. Fraud by fraud, Heep is exposed and undone, and the Micawbers throw in with the emigrants.

story.title: Dora
story.narration: At Highgate the child-wife has been fading. Dora dies in the spring, her little dog in the same hour. David is hollowed out.

story.title: A loose thread
story.narration: One thread is still loose, and it is coming home: a yacht, beating up from the Mediterranean into filthy weather.

story.title: The storm
story.narration: The great storm: Steerforth's yacht is driven onto the Yarmouth sands, within sight of the old boat-house. Ham swims out through the surf to the wreck and drowns; the sea gives back Steerforth's body on the shore he ruined.

story.title: Gathering at the river
story.narration: What is left of the old life gathers on the Thames at Gravesend, where the emigrant ship lies waiting.

story.title: Goodbye
story.narration: David comes down with Peggotty to say goodbye: to Mr Peggotty, to Em'ly veiled on deck, to the irrepressible Micawbers, emigrants all.
On the same tide, the ship weighs anchor and stands down the river for the sea.

story.title: The emigrants
story.narration: Three months under sail: down the Channel, south past Madeira, round the Cape of Good Hope, east across the whole Southern Ocean to Australia. Em'ly at the rail, her uncle beside her, Mr Micawber certain that something, finally, has turned up.

story.title: Grief
story.narration: David cannot stay. Wife, friend and boyhood gone in one season, he leaves England to outwalk his grief.

story.title: Abroad
story.narration: Across the Channel, down through France, over the Alps: months of numb wandering, until he stops in a quiet Swiss valley.

story.title: The valley
story.narration: In the valley, letters from Agnes reach him, steady and certain. The numbness breaks. He begins to write, and finds it is what he is for.

story.title: Home
story.narration: Three years after leaving, David comes home to Canterbury, to Agnes, whom he has loved all his life without once understanding it.
Before the story closes at home, one last dispatch from the far side of the world.

story.title: Port Middlebay
story.narration: The emigrants have made good. Mr Peggotty farms and prospers; Em'ly lives quiet at his side; Mr Micawber, the debtor of Windsor Terrace, is District Magistrate of Port Middlebay.

story.title: The teller
story.narration: Which leaves only the teller of the story.

story.title: Agnes
story.narration: David marries Agnes, and the long book settles into happiness: children, work, the writing of this very story. Of all its faces, hers shines on him to the last.
